subject: Chemistry
title: Ochre: Humanity's First Paint
description: A quick, practical guide to Ochre: Humanity's First Paint.

hook.text: The oldest known pigment is ochre—iron oxide mixed with clay. Humans painted with it 100,000 years ago.

buildup.text: Red ochre gets its color from hematite (Fe₂O₃). Yellow ochre contains goethite (FeOOH). Both are iron minerals.

discovery.text: Heating yellow ochre drives off water, converting goethite to hematite. Prehistoric artists burned yellow to make red.

twist.text: Ochre was used not just for art but for sunscreen, insect repellent, and hide tanning. It was a survival tool.

climax.text: Cave paintings at Lascaux used ochre 17,000 years ago. The same chemistry colors the Grand Canyon today.

punchline.text: Iron oxide plus clay. The oldest pigment is still in the ground beneath your feet.

quiz.question: What gives red ochre its color?
